records.ety: [OE. whinen, AS. hwīnan to make a whistling, whizzing sound; akin to Icel. hvīna, Sw. hvina, Dan. hvine, and probably to G. wiehern to neigh, OHG. wihōn, hweijōn; perhaps of imitative origin. Cf. Whinny, v. i.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Whined (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Whining.]

defs: To utter a plaintive cry, as some animals; to moan with a childish noise; to complain, or to tell of sorrow, distress, or the like, in a plaintive, nasal tone; hence, to complain or to beg in a mean, unmanly way; to moan basely.
quotes: The hounds were . . . staying their coming, but with a whining accent, craving liberty.
Dost thou come here to whine?

defs: To utter or express plaintively, or in a mean, unmanly way; as, to whine out an excuse.
A plaintive tone; the nasal, childish tone of mean complaint; mean or affected complaint.
